text: Two full years later, Pharaoh had a dream: he was standing by the Nile.
Seven fat, sleek cows came up out of the river and grazed among the reeds.
Then seven other cows, ugly and gaunt, came up after them and stood beside them on the riverbank.
The ugly, gaunt cows ate up the seven sleek, fat cows; then Pharaoh woke up.
He fell asleep again and had a second dream: seven heads of grain, healthy and good, were growing on one stalk.
After them, seven other heads sprouted, thin and scorched by the east wind.
The thin heads swallowed up the seven healthy, full heads; Pharaoh woke and realized it was a dream.
In the morning his mind was troubled, so he sent for all Egypt's magicians and wise men, but none could interpret the dreams.
Then the chief cupbearer said to Pharaoh, today I am reminded of my shortcomings.
Pharaoh was once angry with his servants and put me and the chief baker in the guard captain's house.
Each of us had a dream the same night, each with its own meaning.
A young Hebrew, servant of the guard captain, was there with us, and we told him our dreams and he interpreted them.
Things turned out just as he interpreted: I was restored to my position, and the baker was hanged.
Pharaoh sent for Joseph, and he was quickly brought from the dungeon; he shaved, changed his clothes, and came before Pharaoh.
Pharaoh said he'd had a dream no one could interpret, but heard Joseph could interpret dreams.
Joseph replied, I cannot do it, but God will give Pharaoh the answer he wants.
Pharaoh told his dream: I was standing on the bank of the Nile.
Seven cows, fat and sleek, came up and grazed among the reeds.
After them, seven other cows came up, poor, ugly, and gaunt, the worst I ever saw in Egypt.
The gaunt, ugly cows ate up the seven fat cows that came up first.
But even after eating them, no one could tell they had, for they looked just as ugly; then I woke up.
In my second dream I saw seven heads of grain, full and good, growing on one stalk.
After them, seven other heads sprouted, withered and thin, scorched by the east wind.
The thin heads swallowed the seven good heads; I told this to the magicians, but none could explain it.
Joseph said, Pharaoh's two dreams are one and the same; God has revealed what he is about to do.
The seven good cows are seven years, and the seven good heads are seven years; the dreams are one.
The seven lean, ugly cows and thin heads scorched by the east wind are seven years of famine.
It is just as I told Pharaoh: God has shown him what he is about to do.
Seven years of great abundance are coming throughout Egypt.
But seven years of famine will follow, and all the abundance will be forgotten; the famine will ravage the land.
The abundance will not be remembered because the famine that follows will be so severe.
The dream was given in two forms because the matter is fixed by God, who will accomplish it soon.
Joseph advised Pharaoh to find a discerning and wise man and put him in charge of Egypt.
Let Pharaoh appoint officials to collect a fifth of the harvest during the seven years of abundance.
Let them gather all the food of the good years and store grain in the cities under Pharaoh's authority.
This food will be a reserve for the seven years of famine, so the land will not be destroyed by it.
The plan pleased Pharaoh and all his officials.
Pharaoh asked, can we find anyone like this man, in whom is the spirit of God?
Pharaoh said to Joseph, since God has made all this known to you, there is no one as discerning and wise as you.
You will be in charge of my palace, and all my people will submit to your orders; only I as king will be greater than you.
Pharaoh said, I hereby put you in charge of all Egypt.
He took his signet ring off his hand and put it on Joseph's hand, dressed him in fine linen, and put a gold chain around his neck.
He had Joseph ride in a chariot as his second-in-command, and men shouted 'make way' before him; he put him in charge of all Egypt.
Pharaoh said, without your word no one will lift hand or foot in all Egypt.
Pharaoh gave Joseph the name Zaphenath-Paneah and gave him Asenath, daughter of Potiphera priest of On, as wife; Joseph went throughout Egypt.
Joseph was thirty years old when he entered Pharaoh's service, and he traveled throughout Egypt.
During the seven years of abundance the land produced plentifully.
Joseph gathered all the food of the seven years and stored it in the cities, food from the fields surrounding each city.
Joseph stored up grain like the sand of the sea, so much that he stopped measuring it.
Before the famine came, two sons were born to Joseph by Asenath.
Joseph named the firstborn Manasseh, saying God made him forget his hardship and his father's household.
The second he named Ephraim, saying God made him fruitful in the land of his suffering.
The seven years of abundance in Egypt came to an end.
The seven years of famine began, just as Joseph had said; there was famine in all lands, but food throughout Egypt.
When all Egypt began to feel the famine, the people cried to Pharaoh for food, and he told them to go to Joseph and do what he says.
When the famine spread over the whole land, Joseph opened the storehouses and sold grain to the Egyptians, since the famine was severe.
All the world came to Egypt to buy grain from Joseph, because the famine was severe everywhere.
